EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 170 kt upper level jet dove south out of British Columbia into Washington and Oregon by early Saturday November 16th which provided strong lift and precipitation across north-central Idaho into western Montana.  As the orientation of the upper level jet became more northwesterly by the 17th and moved off to the north and east, showers were felt across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from the public reported that 6 of heavy wet snow fell from the evening of Nov. 15 through 10:45 am PST Nov. 16. They reported that the heavy snow created difficult driving conditions. The Pierce RS SNOTEL at 3,080 ft., located 9.2 miles west of Pierce, measured 3 snow and 1.40 of precipitation from Nov. 15-17. The Shanghi Summit at 4,570 ft., located 3.5 mi. west-northwest of Cardiff reported 16 of snow with 2.40 of precipitation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 170 kt upper level jet dove south out of British Columbia into Washington and Oregon by early Saturday November 16th which provided strong lift and precipitation across north-central Idaho into western Montana.  As the orientation of the upper level jet became more northwesterly by the 17th and moved off to the north and east, showers were felt across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dixie COOP observer at 5,740 ft. reported 15.3 of snow with 0.98 of precipitation between Nov. 15-18. A person from the public near Lowell reported that wet snow fell on back-country roads making travel miserable for hunters. The Mountain Meadows SNOTEL at 6,360 ft., located 13.2 miles southeast of Elk City reported 6 of snow with 0.50 of precipitation. The Savage Pass SNOTEL at 6,170 ft. located 5 miles southeast of Powell measured 16 of snow with 1.20 of precipitation. The web cam at Elk City showed snow on state highway 14 during the morning hours on Nov. 16.

CZ_NAME: LOWER HELLS CANYON / SALMON RIVER REGION
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave dropped out of British Columbia, embedded in northwesterly flow and brought periods of blowing snow and snow especially in the Camas Prairie. A plume of moisture with tropical origins interacted with this wave to provide higher snowfall amounts as compared with other northwest waves.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ITD road sensor at Whitebird Hill on US-95 reported 0.25 of precipitation which could equate to around 3 of snowfall.  It reported visibilities below one-half mile at times between 1:46 and 11:16 pm PST, and web-cams indicated blowing snow and snow. The winds were southerly between 10 and 20 mph during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave dropped out of British Columbia, embedded in northwesterly flow and brought periods of blowing snow and snow especially in the Camas Prairie. A plume of moisture with tropical origins interacted with this wave to provide higher snowfall amounts as compared with other northwest waves.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Elk City measured 8.5 of snow that mainly fell during the afternoon and evening on Dec. 20. The Lowell ASOS reported around 0.10 of precipitation each hour from 4 to 8 pm PST which could equate to over 1 per hour snowfall rates, and temperatures ranged between 29-30 deg. F during the event. It recorded a total precipitation amount of 0.69 which could be converted to around 8 of snowfall. Other snowfall amounts: 12.4 Dixie COOP, 9 Mountain Meadows SNOTEL, and 8 2 miles north of Elk City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through eastern Idaho causing widespread 55 to 65 mph wind gusts.   The winds caused a dust storm on interstate 86 reducing visibility to zero.   The visibility caused a 16 car crash west of the Arbon Valley/Rainbow Road exit between American Falls and Chubbuck.   Both lanes of a 12 mile stretch of interstate 86 was closed from 4:45 until 9:45 pm between exits 49 and 61.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread 50 to 70 mph wind gusts occurred behind the passage of a strong cold front.   Taber measured 68 mph for a peak wind with 62 mph at Atomic city, 56 mph at Idaho Falls, and 57 mph at Rexburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Lodgepole Fire was detected on July 20th about 10 miles west of the city of Challis. Many evacuations and closures took place in response. The Mosquito Campground and Mill Creek Campground were closed as well as all roads leading to those areas. Challis Creek Road, Jeff Flats Road and Sleeping Deer Road were all closed. Challis Creek was evacuated on July 22nd and was evacuated at times through the end of the month by the Custer County sheriff. Power lines would occasionally be shut off in the Challis Creek area due to safety reasons for the public and firefighters. Lodge pole pine and Douglas Fir were the main fuels. 787 personnel were deployed to the incident to fight the fire. The fire continued into August until contained on August 24th.   The fire grew to 22,856 acres but significant growth did not occur in August.

CZ_NAME: BIG AND LITTLE WOOD RIVER REGION
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Beaver Creek wildfire began with a lightning strike on August 9th northwest of Hailey.   Some evacuations of Deer Creek west of the Big Wood River were ordered on the 15th.   The East Fork of the Wood River between Ketchum and Sun Valley was evacuated on the 16th.  Up to 2,500 people were displaced.  Highway 75 was intermittently closed.   Rapid growth occurred from August 15th through August 21st as it grew from 44 thousand acres to 108 thousand acres helped by gusty winds and low humidity.  As many as 1,721 personnel were assigned to fight the fire.  All told the fire grew to 111,408 acres by August 26th.  One home, a bunkhouse, and six other structures were destroyed.   12 million dollars were spent on fighting the fire.   The fire was considered contained on August 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most significant flooding took place in Pocatello and Chubbuck in Bannock County, the second major flood in two weeks.   Upwards of 4 to 5 inches of rain occurred in a localized area in Old Town and West Pocatello with more widespread 1 to 2 inch amounts north and east in the greater Pocatello area.    Major flooding also occurred south of Oakley in Cassia County.    Flash flooding also reported in Power County in the Arbon Valley and the Bannock Creek District of the Fort Hall Indian Resveration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inches of rain was reported by spotters in Chubbuck and east areas of Pocatello.   Doppler radar indicated 4 to 5 inches over a localized area of Old Town and west Pocatello.   Several homes and businesses were flooded with some evacuations ordered.  Roads closed in the city were Hiline Road to Griffith Road; Hiline Road to El Rancho Boulevard; the Center Street underpass; Pocatello Avenue; Oak Street westbound to Yellowstone Avenue; the intersection of Pole Line Road and Fir Street; the North Main extension at Praxair; and Garrett Way at the railroad tracks.   Hawthorne Road near Interstate 86 was closed in Chubbuck.   Raymond Park in Pocatello was under water.   2 to 3 feet of standing water near Costco and a roof caved in business near Center Street underpass.   A mudslide was reported at West Custer Street and Highland Boulevard in Pocatello.

Major flooding also occurred south of Oakley in Cassia County.    Flash flooding also reported in Power County in the Arbon Valley and the Bannock Creek District of the Fort Hall Indian Resveration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One basement flooded in the Bannock Creek District of the Fort Hall Indian Reservation near Rooster Rock.   Cars were washed down a road for a half mile.   Water carried debris out of the mountains into the valley and took out fences along side of road from Rooster Rock to the road going to Bannock Peak.   The main impacted area was from Rattlesnake to the Bannock Creek Community Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm impacted the Inland Northwest in two phases.  During the first phase, a deep trough of low pressure dropped southward from British Columbia into the region.  Ahead of the trough, a warm moist conveyor belt of subtropical moisture delivered a shield of isentropically driven precipitation.  Snow levels were driven upward rising above 3000 feet during the first phase of the storm.  The second phase of the storm occurred on December 2nd as the trough sagged into Eastern Washington.  Snow levels fell to valley floors and numerous convective snow showers developed and pivoted around a nearly stationary 700 millibar low.  A majority of the lowland snow came with the second phase of the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from one to three inches in the valleys to up to fifteen inches in the mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm impacted the Inland Northwest in two phases.  During the first phase, a deep trough of low pressure dropped southward from British Columbia into the region.  Ahead of the trough, a warm moist conveyor belt of subtropical moisture delivered a shield of isentropically driven precipitation.  Snow levels were driven upward rising above 3000 feet during the first phase of the storm.  The second phase of the storm occurred on December 2nd as the trough sagged into Eastern Washington. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts varied by elevation.  One to four inches of snow fell below in the valleys below 3000 feet; four to eight inches of snow was observed between 3000 and 4000 feet; eight to fifteen inches were observed in the mountains at elevations above 4000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass spilled into the Inland Northwest between the 6th and 7th of December.  The combination of single digit temperatures and winds on the order of 10 to 25 mph with gusts in excess of 40 mph created wind chills between minus fifteen and minus twenty degrees Fahrenheit in the valleys and up to minus fifty degrees Fahrenheit in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills throughout the Purcell Trench fell between -18 and -22 degrees Fahrenheit.  Wind chills in the mountains dipped between -30 and -50 degrees Fahrenheit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low dropped out of Canada and moved into Idaho on October 28, 2013. At the same time, cold arctic air pushed south out of Montana. A strong wind gradient combined with snow created blowing snow that caused highway 28 to close for a time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Idaho Transportation Department (ITD) weather sensor at Gilmore Summit reported gusty winds to 26 mph with visibilities that dropped below 1/2 mile at times due to blowing snow. The ITD had to close Highway 28 from Leadore south to the Lemhi County line for 11 hours from 7:51 pm MDT on the 28th to 6:56 am MDT on the 29th due to blowing & drifting snow and reduced visibilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level jet and potent cold front bring strong gusty winds to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dent agrimet station recorded a strong wind gust to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure had become established over the Inland Northwest by the 19th of December as a ridge of high pressure anchored over the Eastern Pacific. Meanwhile, a fetch of subtropical moisture topping the offshore ridge became aimed into the region by late on the 19th and majority of the 20th. Strong isentropic processes ahead of a stalled warm front led to a period of moderate to heavy snow across portions of Eastern Washington and Northern Idaho. Most locations picked up between two and four inches with locally heavier amounts observed across the Central Panhandle Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of snow fell across the Central Panhandle Mountains including the Interstate 90 corridor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm eminating out of the Gulf of Alaska spread moderate to heavy snow and strong winds across much of the Pacific Northwest on the 10th and 11th of January. Blizzard conditions were reported in parts of the Magic Valley and along U.S. Highway 93 in South Central Idaho to the Nevada state line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports were received of blowing snow reducing visibility to less than one quarter of a mile, drifts up to 5 feet and cars stuck in snow drifts from Twin Falls to the Nevada state line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A three day period of moderate precipitation from Nov. 2 through Nov. 4, brought up to two inches of precipitation and up to a foot and a half of snowfall across the Clearwater Mountains. Many locations didn't have much snow on the ground and this storm helped to start the 2013/2014 snowpack. A second cold core upper level trough brought another round of snow showers down to the 3,000 ft. level across much of north-central Idaho on Nov. 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pierce COOP observer at 3,185 ft., reported 7 of snow on Nov. 4th while their total precipitation from Nov. 3-4 was 0.93. The Elk Butte SNOTEL at 5,690 ft. located in northwestern Clearwater County reported a storm total snowfall of 11 with 1.20 of precipitation. The Cool Creek SNOTEL at 6,280 ft., located in northeastern Clearwater County reported a storm total snowfall of 19 with 1.90 of precipitation through 1 pm PST on Nov. 4th.  Other snow totals include: 8 Shanghi Summit SNOTEL (4,570 ft.), 14 Crater Meadows SNOTEL (5,960 ft.).

Light snow also fell along across the Camas Prairie with ITD road sensors at both Frei Hill and the Concrete Section reporting slick roads Sunday evening Nov. 3rd through Monday morning Nov. 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Lodgepole Fire was detected on July 20th about 10 miles west of the city of Challis.   Many evacuations and closures took place in response.  The Mosquito Campground and Mill Creek Campground were closed as well as all roads leading to those areas.   Challis Creek Road, Jeff Flats Road and Sleeping Deer Road were all closed.  Challis Creek was evacuated on July 22nd and was evacuated at times through the end of the month by the Custer County sheriff.   Power lines would occasionally be shut off in the Challis Creek area due to safety reasons for the public and firefighters.  Lodge pole pine and Douglas Fir were the main fuels.   787 personnel were deployed to the incident to fight the fire.   By July 31st the fire had burned 20,132 acres and was 15 percent contained.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large moist and hot ridge resided over much of the western third of the US mainland. Temperatures reached well over one hundred degrees in several places in the Salmon River Valley in Idaho. The atmospheric moisture levels were above normal combined with the heat and a weak upper level disturbance triggered slow moving thunderstorms across north-central Idaho. A few storms were able to produce over an inch of rainfall in spots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A forest service employee reported flash flooding that pushed debris across Salmon River Rd. east of Spring Creek closing one lane. Doppler radar estimated 0.78 of rainfall 30 minutes ending at 4:30 pm MDT across the Spring Creek basin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary trough of low pressure along the Pacific Northwest Coastline resulted in moist, unstable, southwesterly flow over the Inland Northwest.  A shortwave embedded within the flow, triggered thunderstorms during the evening hours of August 25, 2013.  Initially, the threat was focusing on isolated severe storms producing large hail.  As the shortwave crossed into the region, a strong squall line crossed from Eastern Washington into northern sections of the Idaho Panhandle resulting in damaging winds and numerous downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter in Fernwood reported hail one inch in diameter.  Time was estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A push of monsoon moisture from the southwestern US helped to initiate thunderstorms across the region on July 16th. One of the thunderstorms produced strong gusty winds that impacted a forest service campground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm in its decaying stage unleashed strong gusty winds that blew over a 24 inch diameter tree onto a camper at the Wilderness Gateway Campground on the Lochsa River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Earlier in the day a surge of monsoon moisture tracked into the region from Nevada allowing for a good buildup of atmospheric moisture.  A 40 knot west-southwest, mid-level jet maximum associated with an upper level wave combined with above normal moisture content (precipitable water values near one inch) and strong heating initiated supercell thunderstorms in the early evening hours over western Lemhi County. One particular supercell brought very heavy rains in a short amount of time inducing debris flow over several of the Mustang burn scars. The Mustang fire occurred in the summer of 2012.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm cell developed over extreme southeastern Idaho county (over 9 kft terrain) and split into two separate supercells as it moved into Lemhi County. The left-moving supercell tracked across the old Mustang burn scarred Colson Creek drainage basin (Mustang Fire from 2012).  According to Doppler radar estimates, this storm unleashed 0.42 of rainfall in 30 minutes across this drainage basin. At one point the cloud tops reached over 50 kft which meant that the storm had tapped into all the potential instability. These torrential rains produced a debris flow that damaged and made Colson Creek road impassable.  The debris flow traveled over several homeowners' properties and damaged a hydro-electric pump causing power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Earlier in the day a surge of monsoon moisture tracked into the region from Nevada allowing for a good buildup of atmospheric moisture.  A 40 knot west-southwest, mid-level jet maximum associated with an upper level wave combined with above normal moisture content (precipitable water values near one inch) and strong heating initiated supercell thunderstorms in the early evening hours over western Lemhi County. One particular supercell brought very heavy rains in a short amount of time inducing debris flow over several of the Mustang burn scars. The Mustang fire occurred in the summer of 2012.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A high precipitation, left-moving supercell caused very heavy rainfall to fall over the Mustang burn scarred Owl Creek drainage basin inducing a large debris flow. Doppler radar estimates of 0.58 fell 30 minutes ending at 7:28 pm MDT over this basin. The debris flow severely damaged landowners' property including water and fuel barrels, irrigation system and several out-buildings. The debris flow also deposited up to 3 feet of rock and mud plus trees over Salmon River Rd.  A few hours later a spotter in North Fork measured 0.50 of rain in 20 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted shortwave trough of lower pressure pushed across the northern Idaho Panhandle during the early afternoon hours.  There was just enough sunshine ahead of the upper level disturbance for surface based thunderstorm development.  Moderate amounts of low level shear combined with the low level energy and dynamics aloft spawned a few stronger thunderstorms the pushed northeastward across the northern portions of the Idaho Panhandle.  One of these thunderstorms was a supercell that produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown over onto Highway 95 at milepost 533.

Moderate amounts of low level shear combined with the low level energy and dynamics aloft spawned a few stronger thunderstorms the pushed northeastward across the northern portions of the Idaho Panhandle.  One of these thunderstorms was a supercell that produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the hail stones were the size of quarters and largest hail stone was the size of a ping pong ball.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning was believed to be the cause for a wildfire in the central Idaho Panhandle mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crescendo fire ignited in very rugged terrain approximately 22 miles south of Avery in the Crescendo Creek canyon.  The fire burned in rugged terrain, which made it difficult for firefighters to access the wildfire.  This fire did not pose a risk to any structures nor was there much of a threat to natural resources.  As such, this fire was monitored through the month and was allowed to continue to burn into August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several reports of 1 inch hail size occurred in Bannock and Bonneville counties.   Lightning damage also reported in Bingham County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch hail reported in city of Chubbuck.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch hail reported at corner of Pocatello Creek Road and Alameda.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning damage occurred in Blackfoot.   One home on S Adams Ave had a window blown out with siding damage and damage to utility lines and appliances in the house.  A telephone was knocked down near the courthouse.   A trailer at the Sage Hill Travel Center was struck by lightning and destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept through the Intermountain West early in the morning on the 23rd. Snow squalls and high winds with damage to trees and power lines were reported across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Idaho Power reported power lines down around the Grandview and Bruneau areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of Pacific moisture brought heavy snow to the Clearwater and Bitterroot Mountains. Lolo Pass on US-12 and Lost Trail Pass both saw periods of heavy snow with 1 to 2 feet of total accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Saddle Mountain SNOTEL at 7,900 ft., located about 1.7 miles west-southwest of Lost Trail Pass on US-93 received 27 inches of total storm snow with 3.50 inches of snow water equivalent. An intense, 4 hour period of heavy snow amounting to 9 inches fell between 4 and 8 pm MST on Dec. 1. At 1:40 pm MST Dec. 1, the Montana DOT reported that chains were required on towing units for Lost Trail Pass. The heaviest snowfall, according to the SNOTEL, appeared to have stopped by 2 pm MST on Dec. 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of Pacific moisture brought heavy snow to the Clearwater and Bitterroot Mountains. Lolo Pass on US-12 and Lost Trail Pass both saw periods of heavy snow with 1 to 2 feet of total accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crater Meadows SNOTEL at 5,960 ft., located 24.8 miles east-northeast of Pierce, reported 17 of total storm snowfall with 4.10 of snow water equivalent. It reported heavy snow between 4 am through 9 am PST on Dec. 1 and also from 1 to 3 pm PST on Dec. 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of Pacific moisture brought heavy snow to the Clearwater and Bitterroot Mountains. Lolo Pass on US-12 and Lost Trail Pass both saw periods of heavy snow with 1 to 2 feet of total accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lolo Pass SNOTEL, located 0.4 miles south of Lolo Pass at 5,240 ft., reported heavy wet snow from 4 am to 8 am PST and from 8 pm to 11 pm on Dec 1 with temperatures averaging around 34 deg. F. It also reported heavy snow from 8 to 11 am PST on Dec 2. The total storm snowfall accumulation was 16 with a snow water equivalent of 2.40. The Dixie COOP located at 5,740 ft., reported 11.8 of snow with 2.36 of liquid equivalent from Dec. 1-3. A person from Dixie reported several power brief power outages when the snow was the heaviest on Dec. 2. This heavy mountain snowfall event could have posed extreme danger to mountain lion hunters driving on forest service roads in the back-country.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The forcing of a 140 to 150 knot northerly upper level jet combined with an influx of Pacific moisture in a post-frontal environment produces heavy snow over north-central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve inches of snow fell in approximately 24 hours in Weippe, Idaho at 3000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The forcing of a 140 to 150 knot northerly upper level jet combined with an influx of Pacific moisture in a post-frontal environment produces heavy snow over north-central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter located 3 miles northeast of Orofino at 3000 feet reported 12 inches of snow in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of upper level shortwaves of lower pressure moved across the area in an unstable northwest flow pattern.  Each disturbance generated some weak isentropic ascent across the Idaho Panhandle.  Lift across the Central Panhandle Mountains and on the Camas Prairie was enhanced by favorable upslope flow into these areas.  The combination of these two factors resulted heavy snowfall accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall reports ranged between three and six inches across the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall reports ranged from 4.2 inches in Nez Perce to between 6 and 7 inches in Winchester.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP observer from one mile north of Dixie at 5740 feet reported a 24 hour snow total of 8.3 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP observer located at Pierce at 3185 feet reported a 24 hour snowfall total of 8.6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The forcing of a 140 to 150 knot northerly upper level jet combined with an influx of Pacific moisture in a post-frontal environment produces heavy snow over north-central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP observer located one mile WNW of Elk City at 4058 feet, reported 12.5 inches of snow in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally moist warm front resulted in heavy snowfall from the Idaho Palouse to the Coeur d'Alene Area. Moisture content with this system was on the order of 180 to 200 percent of what is normally observed for the period this system moved through. A combination of good jet dynamics and strong isentropic ascent provided strong lifting mechanisms for periods of moderate to heavy snowfall rates during the early morning hours on Monday the 7th, which resulted in treacherous driving conditions for commuter traffic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall reports ranged from one inch to up to five inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally moist warm front resulted in heavy snowfall from the Idaho Palouse to the Coeur d'Alene Area. Moisture content with this system was on the order of 180 to 200 percent of what is normally observed for the period this system moved through. A combination of good jet dynamics and strong isentropic ascent provided strong lifting mechanisms for periods of moderate to heavy snowfall rates during the early morning hours on Monday the 7th, which resulted in treacherous driving conditions for commuter traffic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports between two and a half to five inches for the event; a report of eight inches of snow was reported in Worley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of weather disturbances pushed into the region from the northwest.  This disturbance brought with it a moist sub-tropical plume that rode up over an established high pressure ridge in the eastern Pacific.  Primary forcing mechanisms with this system was isentropic ascent and favorable orographic lift due to low level westerly to southwesterly flow.  The southerly component in the low level flow pattern did result in some warm air advection at low levels with snow levels rising through the event.  Many valley locations did see some heavy, wet snowfall accumulations into the overnight hours on the 29th.  Heaviest accumulations were observed closer to the Montana border in the Central Panhandle Mountains where temperatures stayed colder a bit longer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall reports ranged between one and four inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of weather disturbances pushed into the region from the northwest.  This disturbance brought with it a moist sub-tropical plume that rode up over an established high pressure ridge in the eastern Pacific.  Primary forcing mechanisms with this system was isentropic ascent and favorable orographic lift due to low level westerly to southwesterly flow.  The southerly component in the low level flow pattern did result in some warm air advection at low levels with snow levels rising through the event.  Many valley locations did see some heavy, wet snowfall accumulations into the overnight hours on the 29th.  Heaviest accumulations were observed closer to the Montana border in the Central Panhandle Mountains where temperatures stayed colder a bit longer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall reports from four to as much as six and a half inches were reported in the valleys.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passage during the evening hours on the 9th resulted in a favorable northwest flow pattern into the Camas Prairie.  A deep upper level trough of low pressure pushing into the region at the time produced a cold pool that steepened mid-level lapse rates, which resulted in an increasingly unstable environment.  This long duration event continued into the early morning hours of the 11th before drier Canadian air was able to penetrate itself far enough southward onto the Camas Prairie.  By the time the snow ended, Winchester had recorded over a foot of new snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 8.4 inches of snowfall was recorded from the evening hours of the 9th through 1900PST on the 10th, and another 5.1 inches was recorded from 1900PST on the 10th through 0415PST on the 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snow storm hit parts of South Central Idaho on the 8th and 9th of the month leaving 5 to 9 inches of snow across Twin Falls County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 7 incehs of snow had fallen by around 8:30 PM in the Rogerson area on the 8th and a total of 9 inches was reported on the morning of the 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snow storm hit parts of South Central Idaho on the 8th and 9th of the month leaving 5 to 9 inches of snow across Twin Falls County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KMVT received many reports of 5 to 7 inches of snow around the Twin Falls area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front pushes west and south over the Clearwater Mountains towards the lower elevations of the Camas Prairie. It brought several inches of snowfall to the region, caused temperatures to plummet to below normal readings, and caused road surfaces to be slick at times. Sub-zero air temperatures and wind chill values were observed later in the period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter, located 3 miles northeast of Orofino, measured 3.5 of snow that fell with the arctic cold front passage around 2 am PST. They reported that the roads were icy especially in shaded spots during the day on Dec 3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front pushes west and south over the Clearwater Mountains towards the lower elevations of the Camas Prairie. It brought several inches of snowfall to the region, caused temperatures to plummet to below normal readings, and caused road surfaces to be slick at times. Sub-zero air temperatures and wind chill values were observed later in the period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ITD sensor at Elk City reported 3.2 of snow during the day on December 3.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Whitebird Hill ITD sensor at 4,222 ft. on US-95, reported light snow that brought slick conditions during this period. It reported a very low grip level during this period which equates to very slick conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The forcing of a 140 to 150 knot northerly upper level jet combined with an influx of Pacific moisture in a post-frontal environment produces heavy snow over north-central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Idaho Transportation Department's weather sensor, Frei Hill which is located 3.2 miles southeast of Ferdinand at 3664 feet, reported snow with wind gusts to 23 mph. It can be inferred that there was blowing snow at temperatures between 28 and 32 degrees F. The sensor reported 0.77 of precipitation which can be converted by approximation to 8 and 10 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent and moist Pacific storm system slammed into the Inland Northwest from the afternoon hours of February 22nd through midday on the 23rd. A combination of moist isentropic ascent coupled with strong orographic flow resulted in heavy mountain snow across the mountains of Northern Idaho. Snow levels started off near valley floors but eventually rose near 3000 feet as warmer air punched into the region. Valley locations received between one to four inches of snow while eight to twelve inches fell in the mountains.

An arctic cold front pushes west and south over the Clearwater Mountains towards the lower elevations of the Camas Prairie. It brought several inches of snowfall to the region, caused temperatures to plummet to below normal readings, and caused road surfaces to be slick at times. Sub-zero air temperatures and wind chill values were observed later in the period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ITD sensor at Lost Trail Pass on US-93, recorded wind chill values between -20 and -26 F, with wind speeds ranging between 5 and 12 mph. The -26 F value occurred on Dec. 7 at 1:16 pm MST with a wind speed of 10 mph, and also on Dec. 8 at 5:46 am MST with a wind speed of 5 to 7 mph. The Lost Trail Ski Area closed down due to the cold on Dec. 8. The Leadore COOP station observed a low temperature of -29 F on both Dec. 5 and Dec. 6.  The value on Dec. 5 was the 2nd coldest minimum temperature for that day while back in 1972, the record was -38 F. The value on Dec. 6 was the coldest minimum for that day while 1972 was -15 F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent and moist Pacific storm system slammed into the Inland Northwest from the afternoon hours of February 22nd through midday on the 23rd. A combination of moist isentropic ascent coupled with strong orographic flow resulted in heavy mountain snow across the mountains of Northern Idaho. Snow levels started off near valley floors but eventually rose near 3000 feet as warmer air punched into the region. Valley locations received between one to four inches of snow while eight to twelve inches fell in the mountains.

An area of low pressure tracked through southeastern Washington and the Idaho Panhandle during the evening hours of February 7th through the morning of the 8th.  A persistent deformation axis on its northern periphery setup over the lower Idaho Panhandle resulting in several hours of light to moderate snow.  Most locations under this band received between one to three inches of snow with accumulations mainly focusing on grassy surfaces.  The exception was under narrow, heavier bands which set up over the Idaho Palouse and brought upwards of four to five inches.  This included the communities of Moscow, Potlatch, and Viola.  Roads were snow covered during the morning commute but quickly melted by midday.

A strong spring storm system produced heavy snow across the mountains of the Northern Panhandle as a moist warm front moved across the area during the morning hours.  This was followed by a cold front that stretched across the central and northern Idaho Panhandle during the afternoon.  The cold front produced a squall line that stretched from the Coeur d'Alene Area to the Camas Prairie that resulted in severe wind gusts in a couple of spots along this line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heaviest snowfall accumulations occurred in the mountains above 4,000 feet; Schweitzer Ski Resort reported seven inches of total snowfall with this storm system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm system produced heavy snow across the mountains of the Northern Panhandle as a moist warm front moved across the area during the morning hours.  This was followed by a cold front that stretched across the central and northern Idaho Panhandle during the afternoon.  The cold front produced a squall line that stretched from the Coeur d'Alene Area to the Camas Prairie that resulted in severe wind gusts in a couple of spots along this line.

A powerful winter storm pulled through a cold front and heavy snow and strong winds caused blizzard conditions at times throughout southeast Idaho.   Some roads were closed in the Upper Snake and Caribou Highlands.   The heavy snow and winds even reached the Snake River Plain and Eastern Magic Valley where 4 to 8 inches of snow fell with strong winds and below 1/4 mile visibility.   Several mountain locations received over 10 inches of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to 6 inches of snow fell in Idaho Falls with 8 inches in Rexburg.  Strong winds brought several minor accidents on roadways.  Wind gusts up to 40 mph caused visibilities to drop to under 1/4 of a mile.  The winds caused three cross-arms supporting power lines to break around 2:47 a.m.   The break caused a power outage for 181 customers until 2:30 p.m.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm system produced heavy snow across the mountains of the Northern Panhandle as a moist warm front moved across the area during the morning hours.  This was followed by a cold front that stretched across the central and northern Idaho Panhandle during the afternoon.  The cold front produced a squall line that stretched from the Coeur d'Alene Area to the Camas Prairie that resulted in severe wind gusts in a couple of spots along this line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service survey team reported straight line wind damage.  A total of 20 to 30 trees were blown over and another 30 to 40 trees were damaged in a swath of 150 yards wide and half a mile long.  One tree fell on a barn, another on a house, and a third fell on a trailer.  Some of the larger trees that fell over were up to three feet in diameter.  Trees were laying in a southwest to northeast direction.  Winds were estimated at between 70 to 80 miles per hour.  The home owner was in the basement at the time of the damage and was not injured.  A rough estimate was used for the total damage during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow showers developing over the Central Panhandle Mountains resulted in heavy snowfall accumulations above 4,000 feet elevation.  These showers were generated by a weak upper level disturbance moving into the area and by orographic lift due to unstable westerly flow.

A strong cold front moved through the Idaho Panhandle on the afternoon of April 22nd.  Numerous convective snow showers developed in the unstable post frontal air mass.  This resulted in a quick burst of heavy snow at Lookout Pass along Interstate 90.  Upwards of two to four inches of snow fell over the matter of one to two hours leading snow covered roads and a vehicle slide-off which temporarily closed the eastbound lanes for a few hours.

A strong cold front pressed through the Inland Northwest during the morning hours of April 29th. Deep cold advection coupled with tightly packed pressure gradients led to strong winds across all of the Idaho Panhandle with numerous observation platforms measuring sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph and gusts between 40 and 55 mph. This led to numerous power outages, especially in the northern mountain zones, likely due to downed trees and tree limbs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds resulted in power outages to 30 customers in the town of Hope, Idaho and 596 outages in Athol, Idaho.  The cause in Hope was unknown but a downed tree was to blame in Athol.  A NAVY Buoy on Lake Pend Oreille recorded a wind gust to 68 mph however no other observation platforms in the area observed winds greater then 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the end of June 2013, the Inland Northwest experienced a very moist atmosphere for several days.  Constant moistening of the atmosphere from several waves of rainfall and lack of wind or dry air led to surface dewpoints in the 50s to mid-60s and layer precipitable water values between 1.00 and 1.50 inches.  Afternoon heating during the afternoon of June 26th fueled low-centroid thunderstorms that quickly produced tropical-like downpours.  One of these showers moved over the city of Lewiston leading to brief urban flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CWOP mesonet site in Lewiston recorded 0.51 in twenty minutes.  The intense rainfall overwhelmed the city's infrastructure leading to urban flooding and closed streets.  Among the closures were G Street, Adams Lane, and Idaho Street.  Nearly a foot of water was observed on sections of the blue bridge.  The water receded within an hour and no injuries or damage was reported in this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable environment coupled with weak forcing along a passing upper-level front led to scattered thunderstorms across the Idaho Panhandle during the morning and early afternoon hours on June 29th.  In the town of Sagle, two men were injured by a lightning strike while they were laying cable in a trench during the storm.  Reports state that lightning hit the electrical line and went through the buried cable.  One victim had entrance and exit woulds and electrical burns to the lower half of his body.  The second victim had no entrance or exit wounds and appeared to have been hit by the strike's ground current.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The exact time of the lightning strike was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong cold front passed through eastern Idaho and wind gusts of 55 to 75 mph occurred mostly in the Upper Snake River Plain.   The Idaho Falls airport peaked at 67 mph at 3:41 pm.   Craters of the Moon recorded a 69 mph gust.   A 100 year old tree fell at the Tautphaus Zoo in Idaho Falls and trees also fell at Pinecrest Golf course in the city.   Interstate 15 was shut down for hours in between Idaho Falls and Roberts.   Highway 32 between Tetonia and Ashton was shut down due to blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very strong cold front passed through eastern Idaho and wind gusts of 55 to 75 mph occurred in the Upper Snake River Plain. The Idaho Falls airport peaked at 67 mph at 3:41 pm. Craters of the Moon recorded a 69 mph gust. A 100 year old tree fell at the Tautphaus Zoo in Idaho Falls and trees also fell at Pinecrest Golf course in the city. Interstate 15 was shut down for hours in between Idaho Falls and Roberts.  2,460 Rocky Mountain Power customers were without power in Idaho Falls and Ammon for several hours.   Power was restored after 5 pm MDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave dropped out of British Columbia, embedded in northwesterly flow and brought periods of blowing snow and snow especially in the Camas Prairie. A plume of moisture with tropical origins interacted with this wave to provide higher snowfall amounts as compared with other northwest waves.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Idaho County highway reported white-out conditions from Whitebird Grade to Grangeville and from Grangeville to Cottonwood due to blowing snow and snow. They also reported that they received over 30 automobile accident calls. Southerly winds between 20 to 25 mph developed around 2:45 pm PST across the Camas Prairie, as observed by an ITD road sensor located 3.8 miles south-southeast of Cottonwood. The sensors at the Grangeville Idaho County Airport, reported southerly winds sustained between 25-30 mph with gusts to 41 mph between 6:24 pm and 9:44 pm PST. Weather spotters reported 2 per hour snowfall rates in Woodland and in Kamiah around 3:55 pm PST. Other snowfall amounts: 6.5 E Kooskia, 6 1 mi. ENE Woodland, and 4.5 3 mi. NE Orofino.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong rising air associated with the nose of a 50 knot mid-level south-southwest jet combined with near record high moisture levels and an unstable atmosphere triggered wet multicellular thunderstorms over Lemhi County by late afternoon on September 5th. Flash flooding and power outages were the main impacts. To get an understanding how moist the air mass associated with this southwest surge was, the Boise weather balloon sounding recorded the 2nd highest moisture level (precipitable water values) on record on the evening of the 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very wet thunderstorms triggered flash flooding in the Kriley Creek drainage. This debris flow traveled over the northbound lane of highway 93 shutting it down for a period. The BLM road Kriley Gulch Rd was closed in the Kriley Creek drainage due to another separate washout.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong rising air associated with the nose of a 50 knot mid-level south-southwest jet combined with near record high moisture levels and an unstable atmosphere triggered wet multicellular thunderstorms over Lemhi County by late afternoon on September 5th. Flash flooding and power outages were the main impacts. To get an understanding how moist the air mass associated with this southwest surge was, the Boise weather balloon sounding recorded the 2nd highest moisture level (precipitable water values) on record on the evening of the 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Salmon RAWS, located at 5,100 ft. MSL, reported 0.58 of rainfall in one hour. According to radar storm echo trends, the rain could have fallen in less than 30 minutes time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two relatively mild and moist upper level waves dropped out of Canada, and brought snow and slick roads to north-central Idaho. When the snow started, temperatures ranged from sub-zero single digits, to above zero single digits Fahrenheit. Road chemicals were not able to mitigate road conditions at these temperatures, therefore resulting in very slick road conditions until temperatures were able to warm into the 20's to near freezing later on Dec. 10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter located 3 miles northeast of Orofino, measured 6.0 of total storm snowfall. Four inches of that fell on Monday, Dec. 9. The Grangeville COOP reported 1 of snow with 0.09 of liquid on Dec. 9.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two relatively mild and moist upper level waves dropped out of Canada, and brought snow and slick roads to north-central Idaho. When the snow started, temperatures ranged from sub-zero single digits, to above zero single digits Fahrenheit. Road chemicals were not able to mitigate road conditions at these temperatures, therefore resulting in very slick road conditions until temperatures were able to warm into the 20's to near freezing later on Dec. 10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pierce COOP measured a total storm snowfall of 4.8 with 0.10 of liquid equivalent. The Dworshak Fish Hatchery measured 3.5 of snow with 0.12 of liquid. Temperatures started off in the negative single digits and rose to near 20 degrees by the end of the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two relatively mild and moist upper level waves dropped out of Canada, and brought snow and slick roads to north-central Idaho. When the snow started, temperatures ranged from sub-zero single digits, to above zero single digits Fahrenheit. Road chemicals were not able to mitigate road conditions at these temperatures, therefore resulting in very slick road conditions until temperatures were able to warm into the 20's to near freezing later on Dec. 10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dixie COOP measured a total storm snowfall of 3.0 with 0.17 of liquid. The Fenn Ranger Station COOP reported 3 of snowfall. The Lolo Pass SNOTEL measured 12 of snow with 0.50 of liquid.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 170 kt upper level jet dove south out of British Columbia into Washington and Oregon by early Saturday November 16th which provided strong lift and precipitation across north-central Idaho into western Montana.  As the orientation of the upper level jet became more northwesterly by the 17th and moved off to the north and east, showers were felt across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hemlock Butte SNOTEL at 5,810 ft., located 8.1 miles east-southeast of Pierce reported a storm total snowfall of 23 with 2.50 of precipitation. Other totals include: 21 snow with 2.40 of precipitation at the Crater Meadows SNOTEL (5,960 ft.) and 22 snow with 2.50 of precipitation at the Cool Creek SNOTEL(6,280 ft.). The heavy snowfall in the mountains created dangerous conditions for hunters driving forest service roads during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 170 kt upper level jet dove south out of British Columbia into Washington and Oregon by early Saturday November 16th which provided strong lift and precipitation across north-central Idaho into western Montana.  As the orientation of the upper level jet became more northwesterly by the 17th and moved off to the north and east, showers were felt across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Elk Butte SNOTEL at 5,690 ft., located 4.9 miles northeast of Elk River(elevation 2,856 ft.) reported 19 of snow with 2.50 of precipitation. It reported heavy snow from 5 pm on Nov. 15 through 2 am PST Nov. 16, and also from 3 to 5 am PST on Nov. 16. The heavy snow presented dangerous conditions in the higher elevations posing a threat to hunters and other back-country recreation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level jet and potent cold front bring strong gusty winds to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts over 50 mph were reported in the Salmon area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively warm airmass combined with high mid-level moisture and moderately strong mid-level jet helped to trigger thunderstorms across Lemhi County on July 17, 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Leadore Creek RAWS station at 6,150 ft., reported a 60 mph wind gust between 3:36 and 4:36 pm MDT thanks to a decaying thunderstorm cell that passed just to its north.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 170 kt upper level jet dove south out of British Columbia into Washington and Oregon by early Saturday November 16th which provided strong lift and precipitation across north-central Idaho into western Montana.  As the orientation of the upper level jet became more northwesterly by the 17th and moved off to the north and east, showers were felt across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Idaho Transportation Department sensor at Gilmore Summit reported light snow with slick roads between Nov. 16-17. The Saddle Mountain SNOTEL at 7,900 ft., located one mile west of Lost Trail Pass reported 11 of total snowfall with 0.60 precipitation between Nov. 15-17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system brought heavy snow to the mountains of Idaho with several SNOTEL locations receiving in excess of one foot.   4 to 6 inches of snow was reported in the Pocatello metro area.  Several accidents were reported on interstate 15 from Idaho Falls through Inkom.   The right lane of interstate 15 southbound near Inkom was closed for 3 hours shortly after 11 am on the 7th.    Both lanes of interstate 15 southbound near Idaho Falls at around the same time were also closed for 3 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve inches of snow fell at Somsen Ranch and Sedgwick Peak with 16 inches at Wildhorse Divide.   A multiple vehicle accident occurred on interstate 15 near Inkom.  The right lane of interstate 15 southbound near Inkom was closed for 3 hours shortly after 11 am on the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong rising air associated with the nose of a 50 knot mid-level south-southwest jet combined with near record high moisture levels and an unstable atmosphere triggered wet multicellular thunderstorms over Lemhi County by late afternoon on September 5th. Flash flooding and power outages were the main impacts. To get an understanding how moist the air mass associated with this southwest surge was, the Boise weather balloon sounding recorded the 2nd highest moisture level (precipitable water values) on record on the evening of the 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although not under the main multicellular thunderstorm cores, the Indianola RAWS located at 3,529 ft MSL still reported 0.39 of rain in one hour. Considering what other weather stations in the area reported, this amount could have fallen in a much quicker timescale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong rising air associated with the nose of a 50 knot mid-level south-southwest jet combined with near record high moisture levels and an unstable atmosphere triggered wet multicellular thunderstorms over Lemhi County by late afternoon on September 5th. Flash flooding and power outages were the main impacts. To get an understanding how moist the air mass associated with this southwest surge was, the Boise weather balloon sounding recorded the 2nd highest moisture level (precipitable water values) on record on the evening of the 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kriley Creek RAWS, located at 5,200 ft MSL, reported 0.52 of rain in one hour. Radar data reveals that the rain could have fallen much quicker in approximately 30 minutes or less.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Salmon RAWS  located 3 miles southwest of the city of Salmon at 5,100 feet reported a strong wind gust to 69 mph that occurred sometime between 5:36 and 6:00 pm MDT. Weather spotters reported trees down and a power outage in Salmon. A television news station reported on Facebook that over 5,000 people were affected. A person from the public in Salmon commented on this post that power was still out in parts of Salmon at 9 am MDT on September 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 40 to 50 knot mid-level southwest jet maximum combined with unstable conditions over Lemhi County helped initiate a large supercell thunderstorm that brought wind and hail. As the main upper low over Washington and Oregon pushed eastward, thunderstorms developed over Idaho County. One of these storms brought heavy rain that triggered flash flooding over the Sheep Fire Burn area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The US forest service reported that a flash flood had washed out Nut Basin Rd. in the Sheep Fire Burn Area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very large right-moving & long-lived supercell developed across south-central Idaho and tracked north and east across northeastern Lemhi county. A trained spotter in Salmon, Idaho reported quarter size hail. Insurance companies reported at least 200 damage claims to both vehicles and homes from downtown, northwest Salmon and between Salmon and Carmen. An auto body reported fixing 15 passenger vehicles and 20 recreation vehicles caused by hail damage. The hail fell for at least 15 minutes damaging roofs, siding, stucco and windows. One person reported this was the worst storm she had seen in 42 years.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong non-thunderstorm winds downed a tree onto a house in Rexburg and power lines near Parker.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were downed near Parker.   Gusts of 55 to 60 mph measured at Sugar City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A colder than normal mid and upper level low moved from western Washington early on September 25th to southern Idaho before it split into two separate systems. One moved to the north and east into eastern Montana while the other sagged south into Utah by the 26th. On the northern periphery of this system in elevations above 5,000 feet, up to 18 inches of snow fell creating travel impacts, power outages and vegetation damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Idaho Transportation Department estimated 5 inches of snow that fell at Gilmore Summit at 7,186 ft. Webcams revealed snow-covered roads at times during the event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Webcams from Lost Trail Pass (7,090 ft.) revealed snow-covered roads for much of the evening on the 25th through the morning hours of the 26th. The nearby Saddle Mountain SNOTEL located at 7,900 ft. measured 6 inches of snow with 0.90 of snow water equivalent. The pass had to be plowed often during the night which is a rare occurrence and quite possibly due to the unseasonable nature of this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong north jet along the British Columbia Coastline drove a cold low pressure system southward from Western Canada into the Inland Northwest on October 27th.  As low pressure deepened over Oregon and an arctic high pressure cell slide down the Continental Divide, tightly packed northeast pressure gradients developed across the Northern Idaho Panhandle.  This resulted in strong winds throughout the Purcell Trench and adjacent mountains resulting in several downed trees and numerous power outages.  At the height of the storm, over eleven thousand customers experienced power outages across Northeastern Washington and Northern Idaho with the bulk of the Idaho outages occurring in Sandpoint.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 57 was closed five miles north of Priest River due to downed trees on the highway.  Several power outages were observed across the North Idaho Panhandle from smaller limbs and branches falling on power lines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds downed several trees along Highway Three at mile post 55 near the Clarkia Range Station.  No damage was reported but the highway was blocked until the debris could be removed.  Several power outages were observed across area from smaller limbs and branches falling on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent mid-level cut-off low moved from southwest Idaho to southern Lemhi county, Idaho by 6 am MDT Thursday October 3rd and then tracked southeast into southeast Idaho by Thursday night. The storm intensified even more as it ingested Gulf of Mexico moisture over Wyoming, allowing for wrap-around moisture to make it as far north as Gilmore Summit Thursday night. The storm brought snow, blowing snow and slick road conditions to Gilmore Summit and Lost Trail Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Even though it started to snow in the early morning hours of October 3rd, the road temperatures at Gilmore Summit (7,018 ft.) on State Highway 28 didn't approach freezing until 6 pm MDT.  Winds picked up between 17 to 25 mph from the northwest with falling temperatures into the upper 20s. Blowing snow reduced visibilities to less than 1/2 mile at times and roads became slick. The Meadow Lake SNOTEL located at 9,150 ft., 6.5 miles west-southwest of Gilmore Summit reported 0.90 of precipitation which could have equated to at least a foot of snow with temperatures in the mid 20s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent mid-level cut-off low moved from southwest Idaho to southern Lemhi county, Idaho by 6 am MDT Thursday October 3rd and then tracked southeast into southeast Idaho by Thursday night. The storm intensified even more as it ingested Gulf of Mexico moisture over Wyoming, allowing for wrap-around moisture to make it as far north as Gilmore Summit Thursday night. The storm brought snow, blowing snow and slick road conditions to Gilmore Summit and Lost Trail Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Idaho Transportation Department weather sensor at Lost Trail Pass on US-93 at 7,090 ft. reported visibilities less than one quarter mile from 1:45 am to 10:30 am MDT with heavy snow. Chains were required for towing vehicles over Lost Trail due to the conditions through 2:25 pm MDT on Oct. 3rd. The Saddle Mtn. SNOTEL at 7,900 ft., located 1.7 miles west of US-93 reported 9 inches of snow with temperatures ranging from 26 to 28 degrees F. SNOTEL stations located between 7,600 and 8,600 ft. in the Beaverhead and Bitterroot Mtns. reported 9 to 15 inches of total snowfall. A spotter from Salmon reported 10 inches of snowfall with this event at Williams Creek Summit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system dropped out British Columbia, Canada into northeast Washington. This resulted in a mild and moist southerly flow in the Idaho Panhandle. Heavy snow was reported in the mountains of the northern and central Idaho Panhandle. Snowfall reports were much more sporadic in the valleys. Locations below 2,500 feet saw a mix of rain and snow.  Some valley locations saw heavy wet snow accumulations, especially above the 2,500 foot level; however, heaviest snowfall with this system was in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy wet snow resulted in numerous power outages across northern Idaho due to downed power lines and trees onto power lines.  Kootenai Electric Cooperative reported more than 20 outages affecting more than 4,000 customers.

A strong low pressure system tracked across southern British Columbia, Canada. A strong low level jet in the warm sector of the system brought heavy snowfall to the mountains of the northern and central Idaho Panhandle. Breezy winds ensued with the passage of an occluded front in the afternoon and evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted shortwave trough of lower pressure interacted with some surface based instability of near 1,000 J/KG in northern Oregon to spawn squall line that traversed northward across eastern Washington and into the northern Idaho Panhandle. Strong winds mixed down to the surface along the squall line to created widespread damaging wind gusts. Also considered a Haboob, the strongest winds occurred ahead of the parent line of thunderstorms.  Little rain occurred with the thunderstorms, which is indicative of much of the precipitation evaporating before reaching the surface.  This evaporative cooling likely aided in the wind potential from the thunderstorms.  Most of the wind damage occurred near and south of Priest Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several power lines downed across the southwestern portion of Bonner County.

As the main upper low over Washington and Oregon pushed eastward, thunderstorms developed over Idaho County. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from the public estimated ping pong size hail as she was rafting on the Salmon River about 100 yards north of the Salmon River I-93 bridge about one mile south of Carmen, Idaho. Their floating party was unable to reach the beach due to strong southerly winds and proceeded to be pelted by large hail for at least five minutes. She reported vivid black and blue bruises on her arms and legs. Her garden located in Carmen was damaged extensively by the hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter northeast of Carmen estimated that most hail sizes ranged between 0.75 and 1 but there were a few that were up to ping pong size. The hail damaged several fruit trees and tomatoes. His neighbor's wood siding on his house was also damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary trough of low pressure along the Pacific Northwest Coastline resulted in moist, unstable, southwesterly flow over the Inland Northwest.  A shortwave embedded within the flow, triggered thunderstorms during the evening hours of August 25, 2013.  Initially, the threat was focusing on isolated severe storms producing large hail.  As the shortwave crossed into the region, a strong squall line crossed from Eastern Washington into northern sections of the Idaho Panhandle resulting in damaging winds and numerous downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was knocked onto power lines resulting in 41 power outages in Oldtown, ID.  The storm also downed numerous trees across the Priest Lake Ranger District of western Bonner County.  An incident management team was put together to quickly assess and manage hazards discovered in developed campgrounds and recreation sites throughout the national forest given the approaching Labor Day holiday.  Based on the incident map, Navigation, Trapper, Geisinger, Plowboy, and Beaver Creek were among the sites that required hazardous tree removal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving multicellular thunderstorms along a wave of monsoon moisture produced heavy rain and subsequent flash flooding over the old 2012 Mustang burn scar in Lemhi County. A microburst was reported over the Gold Pan fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst with estimated 60 mph winds blew down 20+ trees around the crews at the Gold Pan wildfire. Trees blocked roads and crews had to use chainsaws to get out of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong rising air associated with the nose of a 50 knot mid-level south-southwest jet combined with near record high moisture levels and an unstable atmosphere triggered wet multicellular thunderstorms over Lemhi County by late afternoon on September 5th. Flash flooding and power outages were the main impacts. To get an understanding how moist the air mass associated with this southwest surge was, the Boise weather balloon sounding recorded the 2nd highest moisture level (precipitable water values) on record on the evening of the 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kriley Creek RAWS at 5,200 ft. MSL reported a hurricane force wind gust associated with a wet multicellular thunderstorm. It also reported a 69 mph gust during the 4:51 to 5:51 pm MDT hour. Doppler radar data shows that this gust probably occurred after 5:30 pm MDT. It also showed a wind shift from a west-southwest to a north-northeast direction as the storm passed to the northeast between the 5:51 and 6:51 pm MDT observations. This potentially could be classified as a wet microburst. Power and phone outages were reported in North Fork.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist southwest flow coupled with afternoon instability set off severe thunderstorms with large hail and strong winds across parts of Southwest Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported quarter sized hail around the Jerome, Idaho area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving north-south oriented cold front moving west to east at approximately 45 mph initiated showers and thunderstorms across north-central Idaho. Strong gusty winds up to 50 mph were reported with multiple trees down especially in the Orofino area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous pine and cedar trees were blown down by the wind in the Orofino area. The largest tree diameter was 28 inches. Many trees fell onto power lines causing highway 12 and other roads to close for up to 4 hours. Thirty percent of roof shingles were blown off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving multicellular thunderstorms along a wave of monsoon moisture produced heavy rain and subsequent flash flooding over the old 2012 Mustang burn scar in Lemhi County. A microburst was reported over the Gold Pan fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flash flood occurred in the lower Colson Creek area in northwestern Lemhi County over part of the old 2012 Mustang fire burn scar. Colson Creek Rd. and Road 30 needed to be graded afterwards to permit safe vehicle passage. Up to one foot of debris deposited on roads in several places. Also the increased flow, up to 2 feet in Colson Creek, broke up a temporary earthen dam that emptied a pond.
